title: Contract Formation: Essential Elements Under US Law

content.summary: A legally enforceable contract requires offer, acceptance, consideration, mutual assent, capacity, and lawful purpose under US common law and the Uniform Commercial Code.
content.body: Contract formation requires six essential elements: (1) Offer: A definite promise or commitment to do or refrain from doing something, communicated to the offeree with intent to be bound. Terms must be sufficiently definite for a court to determine breach and remedy. (2) Acceptance: Unconditional agreement to the offer's terms, communicated to the offeror. Mirror image rule requires acceptance match offer exactly; any variation constitutes counteroffer. UCC §2-207 modifies this for commercial transactions. (3) Consideration: Something of legal value exchanged between parties; may be benefit to promisor or detriment to promisee. Past consideration is invalid; pre-existing duty rule prohibits using existing obligations as consideration. (4) Mutual Assent (Meeting of Minds): Both parties understand and agree to essential terms; objective theory of contracts examines outward manifestations rather than subjective intent. (5) Capacity: Parties must have legal ability to contract; minors (under 18 in most states), mentally incapacitated persons, and intoxicated persons may lack capacity. (6) Lawful Purpose: Contract cannot require illegal acts or violate public policy. Statute of Frauds requires written contracts for: land sales, contracts not performable within one year, goods over $500 (UCC), suretyship agreements, and marriage consideration.
key_points: Offer must be definite with intent to be bound
Acceptance must mirror offer terms (common law)
Consideration requires exchange of legal value
Mutual assent judged by objective standard
Statute of Frauds requires writing for specific categories
examples: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. (1893): Advertisement constituted valid offer due to specificity and deposit showing intent
Hyde v. Wrench (1840): Counteroffer of £950 rejected original £1,000 offer; later attempt to accept original was invalid
Hamer v. Sidway (1891): Nephew's forbearance from drinking/smoking constituted valid consideration for uncle's promise

sources.citation: Farnsworth, E. Allan. Contracts. Aspen Publishers, 2004.

temporal_scope.notes: Common law evolves through state court decisions; UCC adopted with variations across states
